news_summary: Today's space news highlights inspiring personal achievements, advancements in rover technology for lunar and Martian exploration, enhanced understanding of space weather effects on Earth, and groundbreaking discoveries about asteroid characteristics.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des exploits personnels inspirants, les progrès réalisés dans le domaine de la technologie des rovers destinés à l'exploration lunaire et martienne, une meilleure compréhension des effets de la météorologie spatiale sur la Terre, ainsi que des découvertes révolutionnaires concernant les caractéristiques des astéroïdes.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan logros personales inspiradores, avances en la tecnología de los rovers para la exploración lunar y marciana, una mejor comprensión de los efectos del clima espacial en la Tierra y descubrimientos revolucionarios sobre las características de los asteroides.

articles.title: From Suriname to Space: Rohit Goeptar Shares His Journey to NASA
articles.summary: Rohit Goeptar was born into a poor family in Suriname, South America, the kind where both parents work three jobs and they still can only provide food and shelter for their family. At around age six, his family moved to California to start a new life. Only two years later, he moved back to South America […]
articles.content:
Rohit Goeptar, an electromagnetic/radio frequency analyst with NASA’s Launch Services Program at the agency’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida, reviews a radio frequency link budget analysis for NASA’s Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope from his office. Goeptar is among the engineers and technicians sworn in as new NASA civil servants as part of NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman’s workforce directive to restore technical core competencies within the civil servants ranks. NASA/Amanda Griffin

Rohit Goeptar was born into a poor family in Suriname, South America, the kind where both parents work three jobs and they still can only provide food and shelter for their family. At around age six, his family moved to California to start a new life. Only two years later, he moved back to South America with his father while his mother stayed in the United States and remarried. When he was 13, he became a U.S. citizen and he and his brothers returned to live with their mother in California.

At 19, Goeptar joined the U.S. Marine Corps where he spent six years as a technical operator. During one deployment to the Philippines, Goeptar helped set up communication systems for individuals who needed to contact their loved ones after a typhoon ripped through entire towns.

“I was lost, the Marine Corps gave me an opportunity,” Goeptar recalled.

While the Marines taught him useful skills, his life had not been the easiest. He lost not one, but two, fathers to suicide, and a short first marriage ended with him being unhoused on the streets of Kissimmee, Florida, for six months. But Goeptar eventually found his way.

As with most underdog stories, there was another person in the shadows behind his rise to success.

“Your brain works in mysterious ways,” his now wife told him a short while after they met. She then filled out college applications for him, and he eventually applied to NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida.

While raising three kids, going to school full-time pursuing a computer engineering and electrical engineering degree simultaneously, Goeptar got the call that changed his life.

“In spring 2025, I was driving to pick my son up from school when a gentleman from Kennedy calls, telling me he’s seen my resume and do I have time for a quick interview,” Goeptar recounted.

He pulled on the side of the road and took part in an impromptu job interview.

Two weeks later, he had an in-person interview with others from Kennedy and two weeks after that, he had a contractor badge at America’s premier spaceport.

After starting as an intern under the Expendable Launch Vehicle Integrated Support, or ELVIS, contract, then moving to part-time until he graduated from the University of Central Florida (UCF) in Orlando, then full-time at the beginning of 2026, Goeptar was one of the ELVIS contractors who applied and were picked up to become civil servants recently.

Rohit Goeptar, an electromagnetic/radio frequency analyst with NASA’s Launch Services Program at the agency’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida, reviews a radio frequency link budget analysis for NASA’s Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope with a colleague. NASA/Amanda Griffin

Now an employee of NASA’s Launch Services Program, Goeptar works with electromagnetic interference, electromagnetic compatibility, and radio frequency. It is his job throughout the entirety of the mission to analyze and ensure avionic boxes or anything electrically powered doesn’t interfere with any other systems. He also ensures independent systems are compatible when brought together. And finally, he conducts model radio frequency link analysis for different rockets and science demonstrations payloads. These may belong to NASA or commercial partners, and he is responsible for ensuring uninterrupted communication with the ground. In his short time at Kennedy, Goeptar has worked on Sentinel-6B, JPSS-4 (Joint Polar Satellite System), and IMAP (Interstellar Mapping and Acceleration Probe) missions.

And as far as his wife’s assessment that his brain works differently, he proved that within a year at Kennedy when he noticed an analytical issue his team wasn’t tracking. Once a rocket launches, it does a pitch, yaw, and roll. The analysis the team had been conducting didn’t account for this movement, which meant it wasn’t as accurate as it could be. He presented his solution to the team lead, and it now enables NASA data and partner data to be much more in sync.

“There is no greater feeling, being able to serve. It’s more than serving the public, it’s serving our country. It’s serving the future of our country,” Goeptar said with tears brimming in his eyes. “Being able to give back to that same government that gave me an opportunity to be where I’m at today. There’s no greater feeling than that.”

Meanwhile, Goeptar’s 11-year-old takes most of the credit for his landing at the space center, a NASA enthusiast, his son believes he spoke it into existence.

Rohit Goeptar, an electromagnetic/radio frequency analyst with NASA’s Launch Services Program at the agency’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida, poses for a photograph with his children. NASA/Rohit Goeptar

“One day he wants to become an astronaut,” Goeptar said with joy on his face. “And I told him I will guide him until the day that I die. Maybe my last mission could be the one my son flies on. I’m not going to stop until that day happens.”

Rohit’s positive streak continues as he recently was accepted into electrical engineering master’s programs at both Johns Hopkins University, and UCF.

Learn more about NASA’s missions online:

https://www.nasa.gov
articles.reasoning: The article tells an inspiring real-life story of Rohit Goeptar, who overcame significant personal and socioeconomic challenges to become a NASA civil servant contributing to important space missions. It highlights his positive impact on NASA's Launch Services Program and his role in advancing space exploration technology. The story has broad significance as it showcases opportunity, perseverance, and public service within a major scientific institution, providing hope and motivation to many.
articles.personality_title: From Suriname to NASA: Rohit Goeptar’s journey to space science
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Rohit Goeptar grew up in a poor family in Suriname, South America. His parents worked many jobs but could barely cover basic needs. When Rohit was six, his family moved to California, but life was still tough. He later became a U.S. citizen and joined the Marine Corps, gaining technical skills along the way.

**What happened** – After serving in the Marines and facing personal hardships, Rohit’s wife encouraged him to apply to college. While studying computer and electrical engineering and raising three children, he got a surprise call from NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida in 2025. After interviews and internships, he became a full-time NASA civil servant in 2026, working as an electromagnetic and radio frequency analyst.

**Impact** – Rohit’s work ensures that the electronic systems on rockets and spacecraft don’t interfere with each other and that communication with Earth stays clear. He helped improve NASA’s data accuracy by fixing an analysis problem related to rocket movements. His story shows how someone from a difficult background can contribute to important space missions and public service.

**What's next step** – Rohit continues to support NASA’s missions, including new satellites and space telescopes. He also plans to help his son pursue a dream of becoming an astronaut. Recently, Rohit was accepted into master’s programs in electrical engineering, which will further his skills and contributions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Rohit Goeptar’s journey from a challenging childhood to NASA engineer highlights determination, skill, and the power of opportunity.
articles.personality_title_fr: De Suriname à la NASA : le parcours de Rohit Goeptar vers la science spatiale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Rohit Goeptar a grandi dans une famille pauvre au Suriname, en Amérique du Sud. Ses parents travaillaient beaucoup mais arrivaient à peine à couvrir les besoins de base. À six ans, sa famille a déménagé en Californie, mais la vie est restée difficile. Il est devenu citoyen américain et a rejoint les Marines, où il a appris des compétences techniques.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Après son service dans les Marines et des difficultés personnelles, la femme de Rohit l’a encouragé à s’inscrire à l’université. Tout en étudiant l’informatique et le génie électrique et en élevant trois enfants, il a reçu un appel surprise du Centre spatial Kennedy de la NASA en 2025. Après des entretiens et des stages, il est devenu fonctionnaire à plein temps en 2026, travaillant comme analyste en électromagnétisme et fréquences radio.

**Impact** – Le travail de Rohit garantit que les systèmes électroniques des fusées et des engins spatiaux ne se perturbent pas et que la communication avec la Terre reste claire. Il a amélioré la précision des données de la NASA en corrigeant un problème lié aux mouvements des fusées. Son histoire montre qu’une personne venant d’un milieu difficile peut contribuer à des missions spatiales importantes et au service public.

**Prochaine étape** – Rohit continue de soutenir les missions de la NASA, y compris de nouveaux satellites et télescopes spatiaux. Il prévoit aussi d’aider son fils à réaliser son rêve de devenir astronaute. Récemment, Rohit a été accepté en master de génie électrique, ce qui renforcera ses compétences.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le parcours de Rohit Goeptar, d’une enfance difficile à ingénieur à la NASA, illustre la détermination, les compétences et la force des opportunités.
articles.personality_title_es: De Surinam a la NASA: el viaje de Rohit Goeptar hacia la ciencia espacial
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Rohit Goeptar creció en una familia pobre en Surinam, América del Sur. Sus padres trabajaban mucho pero apenas podían cubrir lo básico. A los seis años, su familia se mudó a California, pero la vida siguió siendo difícil. Se convirtió en ciudadano estadounidense y se unió a los Marines, donde aprendió habilidades técnicas.

**Qué pasó** – Después de servir en los Marines y enfrentar dificultades personales, la esposa de Rohit lo animó a estudiar en la universidad. Mientras estudiaba ingeniería informática y eléctrica y criaba a tres hijos, recibió una llamada sorpresa del Centro Espacial Kennedy de la NASA en 2025. Tras entrevistas y prácticas, se convirtió en empleado civil a tiempo completo en 2026, trabajando como analista de electromagnetismo y frecuencia de radio.

**Impacto** – El trabajo de Rohit asegura que los sistemas electrónicos en cohetes y naves espaciales no interfieran entre sí y que la comunicación con la Tierra sea clara. Mejoró la precisión de los datos de la NASA al corregir un problema relacionado con los movimientos del cohete. Su historia muestra cómo alguien de orígenes difíciles puede contribuir a misiones espaciales importantes y al servicio público.

**Próximo paso** – Rohit sigue apoyando las misiones de la NASA, incluyendo nuevos satélites y telescopios espaciales. También planea ayudar a su hijo a cumplir el sueño de ser astronauta. Recientemente fue aceptado en programas de maestría en ingeniería eléctrica, lo que mejorará sus habilidades.

**Resumen en una frase** – El viaje de Rohit Goeptar, desde una infancia difícil hasta ingeniero en la NASA, destaca la determinación, la habilidad y el poder de la oportunidad.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing radio frequency wave gently weaving through a stylized rocket launch pad and a telescope dome under a soft sky, with three silhouetted children looking up in awe, symbolizing a journey from humble beginnings to space exploration and the passing of inspiration across generations.

articles.title: NASA Testing Advanced Capabilities for Moon, Mars Rovers
articles.summary: On a bleak stretch of the Colorado Desert in Southern California, a compact four-wheeled rover recently trundled about 16 miles (26 kilometers) with minimal intervention from the team of engineers trailing it. Called ERNEST (Exploration Rover for Navigating Extreme Sloped Terrain), this prototype is being used by NASA to advance both robotic autonomy and the […]
articles.content:
Developed at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory, ERNEST (Exploration Rover for Navigating Extreme Sloped Terrain) is used in a desert field test to help refine mobility hardware and autonomy software that could be used for a potential future long-range lunar rover mission. During the field test, which took place in March 2026 in the Colorado Desert of Southern California, the JPL team deployed ERNEST at all times of the day — including dusk, dawn, and nighttime, when lighting conditions create long shadows like those seen on the Moon’s polar regions.

On a bleak stretch of the Colorado Desert in Southern California, a compact four-wheeled rover recently trundled about 16 miles (26 kilometers) with minimal intervention from the team of engineers trailing it. Called ERNEST (Exploration Rover for Navigating Extreme Sloped Terrain), this prototype is being used by NASA to advance both robotic autonomy and the ability to traverse challenging landscapes.

Developed at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California, ERNEST is 4 feet (1.2 meters) long. Not only can it lift each of its mesh wheels to get past obstacles that would stymie Curiosity and Perseverance, NASA’s six-wheeled Mars rovers, but the prototype also has enhanced independent decision-making capabilities. These mobility and autonomy advances could be infused into future missions that will venture to previously inaccessible areas of the Red Planet or the Moon.

ERNEST serves as a testbed for a potential future lunar rover mission requiring high speeds and extreme distances. In a recent field test, the prototype traveled 16 miles over the course of 37 hours, going an order of magnitude above the top speed at which NASA’s current Mars rovers can navigate. Credit: NASA/JPL-Caltech

In the field, ERNEST served as a testbed for a potential future lunar mission requiring higher speeds and much greater mileage than can be accomplished by current rovers. This technology could be used to inform future designs for exploration efforts on the Moon and beyond.

“This testing is helping us refine the mobility hardware and autonomy software to navigate extreme distances across a wide range of terrain and lighting conditions anticipated on the Moon,” said Issa Nesnas, a principal technologist at JPL who led the recent testing as head of autonomy for a NASA mission concept for a potential future long-range lunar rover.

Engineers from JPL set up illuminators after transporting ERNEST for a pre-sunrise test during a seven-day desert field campaign. NASA/JPL-Caltech

Nesnas’ team is using ERNEST to demonstrate it is possible to build a rover that’s twice as big as the prototype and capable of a long-distance Moon mission. During the recent campaign, ERNEST traveled at speeds up to 0.6 mph (1 kph) over 37 hours of driving, across seven days of intermittent testing. That’s an order of magnitude above the top speed Perseverance and Curiosity can navigate.

“You could do a science road trip across the Moon — or Mars — with this vehicle,” said James Keane, a JPL planetary scientist working on lunar missions.

The initial goal of the team that developed ERNEST was mechanical: to design a relatively simple, low-cost rover that advances the trusted rocker-bogie suspension system featured on every Mars rover since NASA’s Sojourner. This passive system keeps relatively constant weight on all six wheels, thanks to pivot points and struts that enable each one to adapt to the changing surface.

The mobility and autonomy advances developed at JPL for the ERNEST prototype rover could be infused into future NASA missions to previously inaccessible areas of the Red Planet or the Moon. Credit: NASA/JPL-Caltech

On ERNEST, the active suspension lets the rover manage weight distribution among its wheels. Two powered joints in front articulate a gimbal that allows the rover to drive using different gaits like squirming, wheel-walking, and obstacle-climbing. With a clutch mechanism, it can switch between active and passive suspension, which is less terrain capable but more energy efficient. With four steerable wheels, it can drive in any direction, including sideways.

“We started by postulating that we could do better in designing a planetary surface robotic mobility system,” said Hari Nayar, a JPL principal technologist leading the ERNEST team. “While the rocker-bogie system has been very successful over the past 30 years, there’s been a lot of research in that time on mobility and understanding terrain interaction.”

Before arriving at today’s version of ERNEST, the team built two earlier prototypes, each about 2 feet (0.6 meters) long, to test 11 active suspension configurations. In a trailer filled with lunar regolith simulant, they ran experiments at different slope angles over several months before landing on a final design.

Then the team scaled up, including adding a rectangular head mounted on a 4.5-foot-tall (1.4-meter-tall) mast. The hardware was completed in September 2024, but the rover still needed a human operator to joystick it, sending commands to instruct the rover on how to move over obstacles.

In order to train the rover to think on its own, the ERNEST team turned to reinforcement learning, a type of artificial intelligence where the robot learns by interacting with its environment. The Dynamics and Real-Time Simulation Laboratory at JPL developed a high-fidelity virtual testing environment that replicates the rover’s behavior. The team fed the simulator data collected by engineers who documented the response of the actual rover hardware to a variety of terrain types. On a high-performance computing cluster, the team ran many simulations at once, sometimes completing thousands of hours of tests over a single weekend.

After months of virtual training, the ERNEST team was ready to see if the rover could use its new autonomous algorithms to figure out how to drive over terrain features that would halt a passive-suspension rover. They set up an obstacle course with sand ripples, rubble piles, steps, and steep slopes in JPL’s Mars Yard, an outdoor terrain proving ground. Then they watched as the rover maneuvered the terrain on its own. Since then, ERNEST has completed many such courses.

Nayar’s team is starting a new autonomy project which involves integrating the rover’s ability to determine when and how to use its active suspension with longer-range intelligent navigation. The goal is to enable ERNEST to plan an efficient path so that it can tackle surmountable obstacles and circumnavigate hazardous ones. These capabilities could contribute to potential future rover missions encountering formidable landscapes on Mars or more rugged areas of the Moon.

Work on ERNEST began in 2022 was initially supported by JPL internal research and development funds. It is currently funded by NASA’s Mars Exploration Program and the agency’s Exploration Science Strategy and Integration Office in its Science Mission Directorate at NASA Headquarters in Washington. Caltech in Pasadena, California, manages JPL for NASA.

Media Contacts

Karen Fox / Molly Wasser

NASA Headquarters, Washington

240-285-5155 / 240-419-1732

karen.c.fox@nasa.gov / molly.l.wasser@nasa.gov

Melissa Pamer

Jet Propulsion Laboratory, Pasadena, Calif.

626-314-4928

melissa.pamer@jpl.nasa.gov

2026-040
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant NASA technological advancement with the ERNEST rover prototype, which demonstrates enhanced autonomy and mobility for future lunar and Mars exploration missions. This development has broad implications for space exploration, enabling access to previously unreachable terrains and longer-range missions, thus inspiring hope for scientific progress and human knowledge expansion.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s ERNEST rover shows new way to explore Moon and Mars faster and farther
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA wants to send rovers that can travel long distances and handle rough ground on the Moon and Mars. Current rovers move slowly and can’t go everywhere because of tricky terrain and limited control.

**What happened** – In March 2026, NASA tested a new small rover called ERNEST in the Colorado Desert. ERNEST is 4 feet long and has special wheels and suspension that let it climb over obstacles and move in different ways. It traveled 16 miles in 37 hours mostly on its own, moving about ten times faster than NASA’s current Mars rovers. The rover uses artificial intelligence to decide how to move without needing constant human control.

**Impact** – ERNEST’s design and smart software could be used for future missions that explore places on the Moon or Mars where rovers have never gone before. Its ability to drive faster and handle difficult slopes means scientists could cover more ground and gather more information in less time. This rover also works well in low light, similar to the Moon’s polar regions.

**What's next step** – NASA plans to build a bigger rover based on ERNEST’s technology for long-range missions. The team is improving its software so it can better choose paths and decide when to use its special suspension. These advances could help future rovers explore even more challenging landscapes on other planets.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s ERNEST rover prototype uses smart technology and new design to travel faster and farther across tough terrain, paving the way for future Moon and Mars exploration missions.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le rover ERNEST de la NASA ouvre la voie à une exploration plus rapide et plus lointaine de la Lune et de Mars
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA souhaite envoyer des rovers capables de parcourir de longues distances et de franchir des terrains difficiles sur la Lune et Mars. Les rovers actuels avancent lentement et ne peuvent pas aller partout à cause des terrains accidentés et du contrôle limité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En mars 2026, la NASA a testé un nouveau petit rover appelé ERNEST dans le désert du Colorado. ERNEST mesure 1,2 mètre de long et possède des roues et une suspension spéciales qui lui permettent de franchir des obstacles et de se déplacer de différentes façons. Il a parcouru 26 kilomètres en 37 heures, principalement de façon autonome, à une vitesse environ dix fois plus rapide que les rovers actuels sur Mars. Le rover utilise l’intelligence artificielle pour décider comment se déplacer sans contrôle humain constant.

**Impact** – Le design et le logiciel intelligent d’ERNEST pourraient être utilisés pour des missions futures explorant des zones de la Lune ou de Mars jamais atteintes auparavant. Sa capacité à rouler plus vite et à gérer des pentes difficiles permettrait aux scientifiques de couvrir plus de terrain et de collecter plus d’informations en moins de temps. Ce rover fonctionne aussi bien dans des conditions de faible luminosité, comme dans les régions polaires lunaires.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA prévoit de construire un rover plus grand basé sur la technologie d’ERNEST pour des missions longue distance. L’équipe améliore son logiciel afin qu’il puisse mieux choisir les chemins et décider quand utiliser sa suspension spéciale. Ces progrès aideront les futurs rovers à explorer des paysages encore plus difficiles sur d’autres planètes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le prototype de rover ERNEST de la NASA utilise une technologie intelligente et un nouveau design pour se déplacer plus vite et plus loin sur des terrains difficiles, ouvrant la voie à l’exploration future de la Lune et de Mars.
articles.personality_title_es: El rover ERNEST de la NASA abre camino para explorar la Luna y Marte más rápido y más lejos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA quiere enviar rovers que puedan viajar largas distancias y manejar terrenos difíciles en la Luna y Marte. Los rovers actuales se mueven lentamente y no pueden llegar a todos lados por terrenos complicados y control limitado.

**Qué pasó** – En marzo de 2026, la NASA probó un nuevo rover pequeño llamado ERNEST en el desierto de Colorado. ERNEST mide 1,2 metros de largo y tiene ruedas y suspensión especiales que le permiten superar obstáculos y moverse de varias maneras. Recorrió 26 kilómetros en 37 horas, casi de forma autónoma, moviéndose unas diez veces más rápido que los rovers actuales en Marte. El rover usa inteligencia artificial para decidir cómo moverse sin necesidad de control humano constante.

**Impacto** – El diseño y el software inteligente de ERNEST podrían usarse en futuras misiones que exploren lugares en la Luna o Marte donde nunca han ido rovers antes. Su capacidad para moverse más rápido y manejar pendientes difíciles significa que los científicos podrían cubrir más terreno y obtener más información en menos tiempo. Este rover también funciona bien con poca luz, como en las regiones polares de la Luna.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA planea construir un rover más grande basado en la tecnología de ERNEST para misiones de largo alcance. El equipo está mejorando el software para que pueda elegir mejor sus caminos y decidir cuándo usar su suspensión especial. Estos avances ayudarán a futuros rovers a explorar paisajes aún más difíciles en otros planetas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El prototipo de rover ERNEST de la NASA usa tecnología inteligente y un nuevo diseño para viajar más rápido y más lejos en terrenos difíciles, preparando el camino para futuras misiones en la Luna y Marte.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a compact, four-wheeled rover with mesh wheels and an articulated suspension system navigating rugged desert terrain under a soft dawn sky, its mast silhouetted against long shadows that evoke lunar polar lighting, surrounded by subtle hints of rocky slopes and sand ripples symbolizing extreme terrain and advanced autonomous exploration.

articles.title: NASA Mission to Study Space Weather Impacts of Earth’s Atmosphere
articles.summary: NASA selected a mission concept to research how space weather and dynamics within Earth’s atmosphere influence the space environment and help improve prediction capabilities for impacts on crucial technology, such as GPS and low Earth orbit satellites, as well as astronauts in space. The DAPHNE (Dynamic Atmosphere-Ionosphere Explorer) mission will enter Phase B of development, […]
articles.content:
Artist’s rendition of the DAPHNE (Dynamic Atmosphere-Ionosphere Explorer) mission concept. The coloring represents auroras and atmospheric waves in Earth’s atmosphere. Credit: Laboratory for Atmospheric and Space Physics/Mary Tostanoski

NASA selected a mission concept to research how space weather and dynamics within Earth’s atmosphere influence the space environment and help improve prediction capabilities for impacts on crucial technology, such as GPS and low Earth orbit satellites, as well as astronauts in space.

The DAPHNE (Dynamic Atmosphere-Ionosphere Explorer) mission will enter Phase B of development, which includes planning and design for flight and mission operations. It will use identical twin satellites to study how changes in Earth’s lower atmosphere influence our planet’s upper atmosphere, where space weather is manifested.

“NASA is advancing the United States’ leadership as a space weather-ready nation, and by providing new insights into Earth’s atmosphere we can better predict and prepare for impacts in our daily lives on Earth and in space,” said Nicky Fox, associate administrator, Science Mission Directorate, NASA Headquarters in Washington. “As NASA sends astronauts beyond Earth’s magnetic protection to the Moon, Mars, and beyond, DAPHNE will join the NASA science fleet strategically located across the solar system to provide data that will help mission planners predict and mitigate the effects of space weather for the benefit of all.”

The DAPHNE mission’s low-risk high-return concept will provide coordinated, multi-point measurements of neutral winds, temperature, and composition in the thermosphere. The ionosphere and thermosphere regions are where Earth’s neutral atmosphere transitions into the ionized plasma of space. In this thin shell that surrounds the planet, the atmosphere is in constant motion, shaped by the influence of solar activity and changes in the lower atmosphere and in near-Earth space.

Fundamental observations and physical insights from the DAPHNE mission will incorporate lower-atmospheric energy data to advance space weather predictive capabilities. The mission is led by Aimee Merkel from the Laboratory for Atmospheric and Space Physics at the University of Colorado, Boulder.

The mission will be subject to a confirmation review in 2027, which will assess the progress of the mission and the availability of funds. If confirmed, the total estimated cost of the mission, excluding launch, will not exceed $250 million in fiscal year 2023 dollars, with a mission launch date of no earlier than 2029.

The DAPHNE mission was proposed as a concept study in response to the DYNAMIC (Dynamical Neutral Atmosphere-Ionosphere Coupling) mission announcement of opportunity. Funding and management oversight for this mission is provided by the Solar Terrestrial Probes program at NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland.

For more information on NASA’s heliophysics missions, visit:

https://science.nasa.gov/heliophysics

-end-

Abbey Interrante / Karen Fox

Headquarters, Washington

202-358-1600

abbey.a.interrante@nasa.gov / karen.c.fox@nasa.gov
articles.reasoning: The article reports on NASA's selection and development of the DAPHNE mission to study space weather impacts on Earth's atmosphere, which has broad significance for protecting critical technology and astronauts. It provides detailed context about the mission's goals, scope, and potential benefits, representing a meaningful advancement in space weather prediction and planetary protection.
articles.personality_title: NASA launches new mission to better understand space weather effects on Earth
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Space weather, caused by the Sun’s activity, affects Earth’s atmosphere and can disrupt important technology like GPS and satellites. Understanding these effects helps keep technology and astronauts safe.

**What happened** – NASA has chosen the DAPHNE mission to study how changes in Earth’s lower atmosphere affect the upper atmosphere and space environment. This mission will use two identical satellites to collect data and improve predictions about space weather. DAPHNE is now moving into the next development phase, with a planned launch no earlier than 2029.

**Impact** – DAPHNE will help scientists better understand the thin layer where Earth’s atmosphere turns into space plasma, which is affected by solar activity and atmospheric changes. This knowledge can improve warnings about space weather impacts on satellites, GPS, and astronauts, making space travel and daily technology more reliable.

**What’s next step** – The mission will undergo a review in 2027 to check progress and funding. If approved, NASA will continue building and testing the satellites before launching the mission. The data collected will support future space missions and improve space weather forecasts.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s DAPHNE mission aims to deepen our understanding of space weather’s impact on Earth to better protect technology and astronauts in space.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA lance une nouvelle mission pour mieux comprendre les effets du climat spatial sur la Terre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le climat spatial, provoqué par l’activité solaire, affecte l’atmosphère terrestre et peut perturber des technologies importantes comme le GPS et les satellites. Comprendre ces effets aide à protéger la technologie et les astronautes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La NASA a choisi la mission DAPHNE pour étudier comment les changements dans la basse atmosphère influencent la haute atmosphère et l’environnement spatial. Cette mission utilisera deux satellites identiques pour collecter des données et améliorer les prévisions du climat spatial. DAPHNE entre maintenant dans sa phase suivante de développement, avec un lancement prévu au plus tôt en 2029.

**Impact** – DAPHNE aidera les scientifiques à mieux comprendre la couche fine où l’atmosphère terrestre devient un plasma spatial, influencée par l’activité solaire et les changements atmosphériques. Ces connaissances permettront d’améliorer les alertes sur les impacts du climat spatial sur les satellites, le GPS et les astronautes, rendant les voyages spatiaux et la technologie quotidienne plus sûrs.

**Prochaine étape** – La mission sera examinée en 2027 pour évaluer les progrès et le financement. Si elle est approuvée, la NASA continuera à construire et tester les satellites avant le lancement. Les données recueillies soutiendront les futures missions spatiales et amélioreront les prévisions du climat spatial.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – La mission DAPHNE de la NASA vise à mieux comprendre l’impact du climat spatial sur la Terre pour mieux protéger la technologie et les astronautes dans l’espace.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA lanza una nueva misión para comprender mejor los efectos del clima espacial en la Tierra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El clima espacial, causado por la actividad del Sol, afecta la atmósfera de la Tierra y puede interrumpir tecnologías importantes como el GPS y los satélites. Entender estos efectos ayuda a proteger la tecnología y a los astronautas.

**Qué pasó** – La NASA eligió la misión DAPHNE para estudiar cómo los cambios en la atmósfera baja afectan la atmósfera alta y el ambiente espacial. Esta misión usará dos satélites idénticos para recolectar datos y mejorar las predicciones del clima espacial. DAPHNE avanza ahora a la siguiente fase de desarrollo, con lanzamiento previsto no antes de 2029.

**Impacto** – DAPHNE ayudará a los científicos a entender mejor la delgada capa donde la atmósfera terrestre se convierte en plasma espacial, afectada por la actividad solar y cambios atmosféricos. Este conocimiento mejorará las alertas sobre los impactos del clima espacial en satélites, GPS y astronautas, haciendo los viajes espaciales y la tecnología diaria más seguros.

**Próximo paso** – La misión será revisada en 2027 para evaluar avances y financiamiento. Si se aprueba, la NASA seguirá construyendo y probando los satélites antes del lanzamiento. Los datos apoyarán futuras misiones espaciales y mejorarán las predicciones del clima espacial.

**Resumen en una frase** – La misión DAPHNE de la NASA busca entender mejor el impacto del clima espacial en la Tierra para proteger mejor la tecnología y a los astronautas en el espacio.
articles.image_prompt: Two elegant, twin satellites orbiting above a softly glowing Earth, surrounded by swirling, colorful auroras and dynamic waves that ripple through the upper atmosphere, rendered in warm, natural hues with intricate, painterly detail capturing the delicate interplay between Earth’s lower atmosphere and space weather phenomena.

articles.title: NASA’s Lucy Reveals Wobbling, Peanut-Shaped Asteroid
articles.summary: Even small asteroids lead complex lives. During its flyby of the asteroid Donaldjohanson last year, NASA’s Lucy spacecraft revealed the asteroid to be a wobbly, peanut-shaped body that has undergone a lot of activity in its relatively short history. Formed as fragments coalesced after a violent collision 155 million years ago, the asteroid was transformed by the […]
articles.content:
Even small asteroids lead complex lives. During its flyby of the asteroid Donaldjohanson last year, NASA’s Lucy spacecraft revealed the asteroid to be a wobbly, peanut-shaped body that has undergone a lot of activity in its relatively short history. Formed as fragments coalesced after a violent collision 155 million years ago, the asteroid was transformed by the small but inexorable force of the Sun’s radiation, all while retaining signs of the brief presence of liquid water in its distant past.

Zooming through the main asteroid belt toward one of the Jupiter Trojan asteroid groups, the Lucy spacecraft collected the first close-up images and other data at Donaldjohanson on April 20, 2025, as it passed 650 miles away from the asteroid. The data revealed that, instead of spinning simply around one axis like most other asteroids and planets, Donaldjohanson has a more complicated two-axis rotation. Scientists also saw Donaldjohanson’s peanut shape and the craters and ridges on its surface.

To view this video please enable JavaScript, and consider upgrading to a web browser that supports HTML5 video A timelapse video made from images taken by NASA’s Lucy spacecraft as it approached the asteroid Donaldjohanson on April 20, 2025. The L’LORRI (Lucy Long Range Reconnaissance Imager) instrument, the spacecraft’s high-resolution black-and-white imager, collected these images over two hours as the spacecraft rapidly closed in on the asteroid from an initial separation of more than 58,000 miles (93,000 km), until the spacecraft passed a mere 650 miles (1000 km) from the 5-mile- (8 km-) wide asteroid. NASA/Goddard/SwRI/JHU-APL

Lucy’s encounter with the asteroid was planned as a dress rehearsal for the spacecraft and mission team before its primary asteroid encounters, which begin with Lucy’s flyby of the Trojan asteroid Eurybates on Aug. 12, 2027. The instruments performed as expected, and, as a bonus, scientists got a rare opportunity to study a previously unexplored asteroid up close and to compare it to two asteroids with similar compositions but different histories: Bennu, the target of NASA’s OSIRIS-REx sample-return mission, and Ryugu, the site of JAXA’s (Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency) Hayabusa2 sample-return mission.

Here’s what Lucy’s science team has learned so far from Lucy’s encounter with Donaldjohanson, as reported on June 18 in the journal Science.

Wobbling rotation

With Earth-based telescopes, observers saw fluctuations in the light Donaldjohanson reflects, regular patterns of peaks and valleys, typical of an elongated object rotating once every 10.5 Earth days. But Lucy’s data revealed another pattern: Donaldjohanson appears to be rotating like a wobbly top. Paper authors reported that the asteroid rotates end-over-end once every 10.5 Earth days, and wobbles back and forth around its long axis once every 26.5 days.

To view this video please enable JavaScript, and consider upgrading to a web browser that supports HTML5 video The asteroid Donaldjohanson is shown slowly rotating in a tumbling, non-principal axis motion, with its angular momentum vector and rotation axes indicated. The surface is colored by gravity slope, which measures the angle between the local surface and the direction of gravity. Higher values (warmer colors) indicate steeper terrain relative to the local gravitational pull. Regions with limited stereo image coverage have been masked out where the shape model is less well constrained. Kel Elkins/NASA’s Science Visualization Studio/DLR

Peanut shape

While the Earth-based observations hinted at Donaldjohanson’s elongated shape, the Lucy flyby revealed a “bilobate” structure: two lobes connected by a neck, like a peanut. These lobes are likely two fragments from an asteroid collision that gently came together afterward by their mutual gravity.

Donaldjohanson likely rotated at least 10 times faster when it formed, having slowed to its current rate in the last 20 to 60 million years, the team estimates. As it slowed, the balance between the centrifugal force pushing things apart and gravity pulling things together changed and loose rocky material slid down slopes creating the worn-down appearance of many craters, as the flyby images showed.

The paper’s authors say that the asteroid’s slowing rotation is likely caused by a subtle consequence of solar heating known as the YORP effect. Each part of the asteroid’s Sun-warmed surface radiates heat away as infrared light, and that radiation imparts a tiny recoil force to the surface. Because the asteroid’s shape isn’t symmetric, this results in a net torque, or twist, that can change the asteroid’s rotation. Thus, YORP can slow asteroid spins down or speed them up, as in the case of Bennu (once every four hours) and Ryugu (once about every seven hours), which both likely used to rotate much slower than they do today.

Fleeting water

As it passed by Donaldjohanson at 30,000 mph, Lucy recorded the signatures of iron-rich clay minerals on the surface. These clays must have formed in the distant past with the help of liquid water. However, the exposure must have been brief, Lucy scientists concluded, because iron in clays tends to be replaced with other elements, such as magnesium, as water lingers.

Indeed, scientists saw magnesium-rich clays at Bennu and Ryugu, which suggested prolonged water exposure, perhaps lasting millions of years, when they were still part of larger asteroids.

This difference in water exposure history, and other characteristics, may mean that the parent bodies of these asteroids formed at different times or in different regions of the solar system before relocating to the main belt.

Compare, contrast

Donaldjohanson is thought to be made from rocky remnants of a larger, carbon- and water-rich asteroid that collided with another object in the main asteroid belt. Bennu and Ryugu are thought to have formed in the same way and in the same region.

But Donaldjohanson is different. At 155 million years old, it is much younger than Bennu and Ryugu, which formed 1 to 2 billion years ago. Donaldjohanson also has remained in the asteroid belt since birth, while its wandering cousins migrated into orbits around the Sun that bring them close to Earth’s orbit about once a year (which made them perfect close targets for sample return missions).

During its April 20, 2025, encounter with the main-belt asteroid Donaldjohanson, NASA’s Lucy spacecraft discovered evidence for iron-rich clays on the surface using its infrared spectrometer. These clays, which are similar to those found in carbon-rich meteorites such as QUE 97990, indicate that water was briefly present in the asteroid during the distant past. NASA/Goddard/SwRI/Dan Gallagher

"It’s helpful for scientists to compare Donaldjohanson with asteroids like Bennu and Ryugu, which are seemingly similar asteroids, because every subtle difference is another clue to our origin story,” said Simone Marchi, Lucy deputy principal investigator and lead author of the study at the Boulder, Colorado, office of the Southwest Research Institute.

“Once we start learning more about the Trojans, a completely different population of space rocks with very different histories, our understanding of solar system formation is destined to be challenged,” said Marchi.

Named after a fossilized skeleton of a human ancestor discovered in Ethiopia in 1974, NASA’s Lucy will be the first mission to explore Jupiter’s Trojan asteroids, a population of well-preserved space rocks that formed early in our solar system’s history and could help scientists understand how the planets formed and moved around before settling in their current configuration.

Download story graphics from NASA's Scientific Visualization Studio.

About Lucy:

Lucy’s principal investigator is based out of the Boulder, Colorado, branch of Southwest Research Institute, headquartered in San Antonio. NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland, provides overall mission management, systems engineering, and safety and mission assurance. Lockheed Martin Space in Littleton, Colorado, built the spacecraft. Lucy is the 13th mission in NASA’s Discovery Program. NASA’s Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville, Alabama, manages the Discovery Program for the agency’s Science Mission Directorate in Washington.

For more information on NASA’s Lucy mission, visit:

By Lonnie Shekhtman

NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center, Greenbelt, Md.

and

Katherine Kretke

Southwest Research Institute, Boulder, Colo.

Media Contacts:

Karen Fox / Molly Wasser

Headquarters, Washington

240-285-5155 / 240-419-1732

karen.c.fox@nasa.gov / molly.l.wasser@nasa.gov

Sarah Frazier

NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center, Greenbelt, Md.

202-853-7191

sarah.frazier@nasa.gov
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery by NASA's Lucy spacecraft revealing detailed characteristics of the asteroid Donaldjohanson, including its wobbling rotation, peanut shape, and evidence of past water presence. This contributes to our understanding of solar system formation, which has broad scientific and educational impact. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a real event with meaningful implications for planetary science.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Lucy spacecraft uncovers surprising features of asteroid Donaldjohanson
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA launched the Lucy spacecraft to study asteroids, especially those near Jupiter called Trojans. These asteroids hold clues about how our solar system formed billions of years ago.

**What happened** – On April 20, 2025, Lucy flew close to a main-belt asteroid named Donaldjohanson, about 650 miles away. It took detailed pictures and measurements. Scientists discovered that Donaldjohanson has a peanut-like shape and spins in a wobbling motion, unlike most asteroids that spin smoothly. The asteroid formed from fragments after a collision 155 million years ago and shows signs that liquid water was once briefly present on its surface.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows that even small asteroids have complex histories. Donaldjohanson’s wobbling spin and peanut shape tell scientists how it changed over time, including a slow-down caused by sunlight pushing it in a tiny but steady way. Finding iron-rich clays means water existed there long ago, but only for a short time. Comparing Donaldjohanson to other asteroids like Bennu and Ryugu helps scientists understand differences in their origins and how the solar system evolved.

**What's next step** – Lucy will continue its journey to study Trojan asteroids near Jupiter, starting with a flyby of Eurybates in 2027. These future studies will add more pieces to the puzzle of how planets and asteroids formed and moved in the early solar system.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s Lucy mission revealed that asteroid Donaldjohanson has a unique wobbling spin and peanut shape, offering new clues about asteroid history and solar system formation.
articles.personality_title_fr: La sonde Lucy de la NASA révèle les caractéristiques surprenantes de l’astéroïde Donaldjohanson
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA a lancé la sonde Lucy pour étudier les astéroïdes, notamment ceux proches de Jupiter appelés les Troyens. Ces astéroïdes contiennent des indices sur la formation de notre système solaire il y a des milliards d’années.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 20 avril 2025, Lucy est passée près d’un astéroïde de la ceinture principale nommé Donaldjohanson, à environ 1 000 kilomètres. Elle a pris des photos détaillées et fait des mesures. Les scientifiques ont découvert que Donaldjohanson a une forme de cacahuète et tourne en vacillant, contrairement à la plupart des astéroïdes qui tournent de manière régulière. Cet astéroïde s’est formé il y a 155 millions d’années après une collision et montre des signes qu’il y a eu de l’eau liquide à sa surface, mais seulement brièvement.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que même les petits astéroïdes ont des histoires complexes. La rotation vacillante et la forme de cacahuète de Donaldjohanson expliquent comment il a changé au fil du temps, notamment un ralentissement provoqué par la lumière du Soleil qui exerce une faible poussée constante. La présence d’argiles riches en fer indique que de l’eau a existé là-bas il y a longtemps, mais pour une courte période. Comparer Donaldjohanson à d’autres astéroïdes comme Bennu et Ryugu aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre leurs origines et l’évolution du système solaire.

**Prochaine étape** – Lucy poursuivra son voyage pour étudier les astéroïdes Troyens près de Jupiter, avec un survol d’Eurybates prévu en 2027. Ces prochaines études permettront d’en apprendre davantage sur la formation et le déplacement des planètes et des astéroïdes au début du système solaire.

**Phrase clé** – La mission Lucy de la NASA a révélé que l’astéroïde Donaldjohanson a une rotation vacillante unique et une forme de cacahuète, offrant de nouveaux indices sur l’histoire des astéroïdes et la formation du système solaire.
articles.personality_title_es: La nave Lucy de la NASA descubre características sorprendentes del asteroide Donaldjohanson
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA lanzó la nave Lucy para estudiar asteroides, especialmente los llamados troyanos que orbitan cerca de Júpiter. Estos asteroides guardan pistas sobre cómo se formó nuestro sistema solar hace miles de millones de años.

**Qué pasó** – El 20 de abril de 2025, Lucy se acercó a un asteroide en el cinturón principal llamado Donaldjohanson, a unos 1,000 kilómetros. Tomó imágenes detalladas y midió sus características. Los científicos descubrieron que Donaldjohanson tiene forma de maní y gira con un movimiento tambaleante, diferente a la mayoría de los asteroides que giran de forma estable. Este asteroide se formó hace 155 millones de años tras una colisión y muestra señales de que hubo agua líquida en su superficie, pero solo por un tiempo corto.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque muestra que incluso los asteroides pequeños tienen historias complejas. La rotación tambaleante y la forma de maní de Donaldjohanson explican cómo ha cambiado con el tiempo, incluyendo una desaceleración causada por la luz del Sol que ejerce una pequeña fuerza constante. Encontrar arcillas ricas en hierro indica que hubo agua en el pasado, aunque por poco tiempo. Comparar Donaldjohanson con otros asteroides como Bennu y Ryugu ayuda a los científicos a entender mejor sus orígenes y la evolución del sistema solar.

**Próximo paso** – Lucy continuará su viaje para estudiar los asteroides troyanos cerca de Júpiter, comenzando con un sobrevuelo de Eurybates en 2027. Estos estudios futuros ayudarán a comprender cómo se formaron y movieron los planetas y asteroides en el sistema solar temprano.

**Frase clave** – La misión Lucy de la NASA reveló que el asteroide Donaldjohanson tiene un giro tambaleante y forma de maní, dando nuevas pistas sobre la historia de los asteroides y la formación del sistema solar.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a large, peanut-shaped asteroid gently tumbling through space with subtle wobbling motion, its textured surface showing craters and ridges under soft sunlight, accompanied by a sleek, symbolic spacecraft nearby capturing its image, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and deep space blues.
